title: Tipping Points Explorer
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive stability landscape showing how increasing global temperature triggers successive climate tipping points with nonlinear system responses and hysteresis

educational.topic: Climate Tipping Points and Nonlinear System Dynamics
learningObjectives: Predict how increasing global temperature activates successive tipping points and explain why the system response is nonlinear
Define hysteresis and explain why some climate changes are effectively irreversible
Evaluate the consequences of crossing specific climate tipping points
prerequisites: Basic understanding of climate change and greenhouse effect

keyQuestions: What does it mean for a climate system to cross a tipping point?
Why can't we simply reverse climate tipping points by reducing temperature?
How do positive feedback loops relate to tipping point dynamics?
Why did the Paris Agreement set temperature targets at 1.5 and 2.0 degrees C?
How might one tipping point cascade into triggering others?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think climate change effects are linear and proportional to temperature increase, when many critical changes are threshold-driven
Students may believe all climate changes are reversible if we reduce emissions, not understanding hysteresis
Students sometimes think tipping points only affect one part of the Earth system, not realizing cascading effects
assessmentOpportunities: Have students create a tipping point threshold chart recording activation temperatures and consequences
Ask students to research and present on cascading effects from a specific tipping element
Students write a policy brief using simulation evidence to argue for specific temperature targets
